one_line_takeaway: TAGBD shows that poisoning only node TEXT (no edge/topology changes) in text-attributed graphs is sufficient to implant near-100% ASR backdoors that survive structural defenses (Prune, OD) which neutralize prior topology-based graph backdoor attacks.
key_finding: TAGBD achieves near-perfect attack success with a text-only, structure-preserving attack: on Table II, Overwriting reaches ASR of 100.00% (Cora, no defense), 99.59% (Pubmed, no defense), and 99.52% (ArXiv, no defense), versus the strongest baseline (structural, DPGBA) at 93.25% (Cora), 92.55% (Pubmed), 96.64% (ArXiv). Under the Prune defense, most baselines collapse (e.g., GTA drops to 0.25% ASR on Cora, DPGBA drops to 19.47% on Cora and 0.00% on ArXiv) while TAGBD Overwriting stays at 100.00%/99.85%/99.96% ASR on Cora/Pubmed/ArXiv respectively -- i.e., structural defenses that hurt topology-based baselines have essentially no effect on TAGBD because it never touches topology. Under OD defense, GTA and UGBA also crash on some datasets (e.g., UGBA 0.00% ASR on Cora under OD) while TAGBD retains 100.00%/99.85%/99.73% ASR. Clean accuracy is largely preserved (e.g., 86.72-93.10% CA for Overwriting across settings, though ArXiv CA for Overwriting is notably lower, ~59-73%, reflecting the harder 40-class task). The paper also finds the attack is budget-efficient (near-saturating ASR with only ~20 poisoned nodes on Pubmed and ~200 on ArXiv, per Fig. 2), transfers across five target GNN architectures on ArXiv with Overwriting ASR in the 98.89-99.62% range (Table III), and that stronger text encoder-decoders (Sonar > GTR-T5 > BOW) improve both ASR and text fluency (Table IV: Sonar reaches 100.00%/99.96% ASR with PPL 175.23/95.45, versus BOW at 50.92%/18.82% ASR with PPL 211.73/220.33), indicating the trigger quality is coupled to the strength of the underlying text-generation backbone.
core_contribution: The paper proposes TAGBD (Text-Attributed Graph BackDoor), a training-time backdoor poisoning attack against GNNs on text-attributed graphs (TAGs) that edits ONLY node text and leaves graph topology completely unchanged. TAGBD has three components: (1) an uncertainty-guided node selection strategy that trains a surrogate GNN on labeled nodes and picks the highest-entropy unlabeled nodes (subject to a minimum-class-coverage constraint) as poisoning targets; (2) TextTrojan, a neural trigger generator that takes graph-aware node embeddings (from the surrogate GNN, i.e. embeddings that already reflect neighborhood aggregation) and produces poisoned text embeddings, jointly trained with a shadow GNN using a combined attack loss, clean-utility loss, and cosine-similarity semantic-regularization loss; (3) two injection modes -- Overwriting (replace the node's original text entirely) and Appending (concatenate the generated trigger to the original text) -- that trade off attack strength against stealth. The paper explicitly frames its own contribution as showing that a text-only channel (no edge/feature edits) is sufficient to backdoor TAG models, in contrast to prior structural graph-backdoor work.
threat_model: Gray-box, inductive node classification on TAGs. The attacker can access the training graph (node texts and topology) and a subset of labeled nodes, but does NOT know the target GNN's architecture, parameters, or training procedure -- so the attack must work through poisoned data alone (surrogate-based), not white-box optimization against the deployed model. The attacker is restricted to editing node TEXT only; graph structure (edges) is left untouched, reflecting the paper's stated realism argument that editing text (profile descriptions, posts, abstracts) is easier and less visible than adding/rewiring edges. Attack goal: any test node containing the (instance-dependent, generator-produced) trigger is classified into an attacker-chosen target class yt, while clean-node accuracy stays high so the poisoned model is not obviously degraded. The defender is assumed to evaluate primarily via standard predictive utility and possibly structural defenses (edge pruning, structural-outlier detection) -- the paper explicitly tests whether a text-only attacker can bypass defenses built around structural irregularities.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several caveats: (1) Overwriting, the strongest-ASR variant, produces text with higher perplexity than clean text (case studies in Table V/VI show visibly repetitive/degenerate generated text, e.g., a phrase repeated ~5 times verbatim), which is a plausible detectability weakness even though the paper frames Appending as the stealth-oriented mitigation; (2) on ArXiv, clean accuracy for Overwriting is noticeably lower (roughly 56-73% CA depending on defense) than on Cora/Pubmed, suggesting a real utility cost on the largest, hardest (40-class) benchmark that the paper does not foreground as a limitation; (3) the gray-box threat model still assumes the attacker can access/label a portion of the training graph and train a surrogate GNN of a compatible type (GCN) -- transferability was tested across five GNN families but the surrogate itself is fixed to a 2-layer GCN, so generalization to very different backbone families is not separately established; (4) only two structural defenses (Prune, OD) are evaluated; no content-based or text-anomaly defenses (e.g., perplexity filtering, semantic outlier detection on text) are tested against TAGBD, despite the paper itself arguing that stronger text encoders/decoders shift the risk toward content-aware defenses being necessary -- this leaves the paper's central claim (that structural defenses are insufficient) unpaired with an evaluation of the natural counter-defense class it motivates.

datasets_used: Cora (2,708 nodes, 10,556 edges, 7 classes, avg 138 / max 763 words per node text)
Pubmed (19,717 nodes, 88,648 edges, 3 classes, avg 247 / max 895 words per node text)
ArXiv (169,343 nodes, 1,166,243 edges, 40 classes, avg 421 / max 1414 words per node text)
all three from the TAGLAS benchmark suite
quantitative_results: Cora, no defense: TAGBD Overwriting ASR 100.00% / CA 86.72%; Appending ASR 99.63% / CA 86.72%; best baseline DPGBA ASR 93.25% / CA 79.22%
Cora, Prune defense: Overwriting ASR 100.00% / CA 87.82%; Appending ASR 99.37% / CA 85.61%; GTA collapses to 0.25% ASR; DPGBA collapses to 19.47% ASR
Cora, OD defense: Overwriting ASR 100.00% / CA 87.45%; Appending ASR 95.20% / CA 91.14%; UGBA collapses to 0.00% ASR
Pubmed, no defense: Overwriting ASR 99.59% / CA 93.05%; Appending ASR 98.59% / CA 93.05%
ArXiv, no defense: Overwriting ASR 99.52% / CA 59.74%; Appending ASR 96.47% / CA 64.71%
ArXiv, Prune defense: Overwriting ASR 99.96% / CA 73.00%; DPGBA collapses to 0.00% ASR

Transferability on ArXiv (Table III): Overwriting ASR ranges 98.89%-99.62% (CA 83.03-87.45%) across GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GraphTransformer, RobustGCN; Appending ASR ranges 91.94%-99.15% (CA 61.55-69.83%)

Encoder-decoder ablation on Cora (Table IV): BOW Overwriting ASR 50.92% / CA 88.39% / PPL 211.73; GTR-T5 Overwriting ASR 98.52% / CA 87.08% / PPL 341.62; Sonar Overwriting ASR 100.00% / CA 87.22% / PPL 175.23; Sonar Appending ASR 99.96% / CA 76.35% / PPL 95.45
Poisoning budget of 1% of unlabeled training nodes (~27 nodes on Cora) already yields strong ASR; near-saturation ASR with ~20 poisoned nodes on Pubmed and ~200 on ArXiv
